figures.gloss: A research psychologist at Columbia's College of Physicians and Surgeons — professionally skeptical and a self-described atheist — who in October 1965 began hearing a rapid inner dictation ('This is a course in miracles. Please take notes.') and, with her colleague William Thetford typing, took seven years of it down in shorthand.
figures.facet: The corpus's most reluctant prophet: she never joined the movement her notebooks created, remained privately conflicted about the material, and kept her authorship anonymous for the rest of her life.
figures.provenance: Kenneth Wapnick, 'Absence from Felicity' (1991); Foundation for Inner Peace preface and scribal history; William Thetford's accounts.

figures.gloss: The three-volume curriculum published by the Foundation for Inner Peace in 1976: a Text of theory, a Workbook of 365 daily lessons, and a Manual for Teachers — a complete self-study technology whose stated aim is not to teach love but to remove 'the blocks to the awareness of love's presence'.
figures.facet: Christian vocabulary run through a psychoanalytic lens to deliver a non-dual metaphysics: sin becomes error, atonement becomes undoing, the crucifixion a teaching demonstration — heresy composed in the mother tongue of the church.
figures.provenance: A Course in Miracles (Foundation for Inner Peace, 1976); Schucman-Thetford scribal history; D. Patrick Miller's documentary histories of the Course.

figures.gloss: The belief in separation from God, which the Course treats as the manufacturer of the entire perceptual world: time, space, bodies, and death are its defensive dream, spun to keep the separation seemingly real and God seemingly absent.
figures.facet: A demiurge with no ontology: the Course's adversary makes the world, as in Gnosticism, yet is explicitly nothing — an insane thought taken seriously, to be dissolved not by battle but by withdrawal of belief.
figures.provenance: A Course in Miracles, Text (esp. the early chapters on the ego's dynamics); Kenneth Wapnick's exegesis.

figures.gloss: God's Answer to the separation: the Voice placed within the dreaming mind that still remembers reality, reinterpreting everything the ego made — every grievance, body, and event — as a lesson leading home.
figures.facet: The psychopomp relocated inside the psyche: this guide ferries no souls across a literal threshold but escorts perception itself from nightmare to 'the real world', the last stop before God.
figures.provenance: A Course in Miracles, Text chapter 5 and the Manual for Teachers (Foundation for Inner Peace, 1976).

figures.gloss: The Course's single practice and entire soteriology: the recognition that what your brother 'did' never happened in reality, so nothing needs pardoning — forgiveness 'sees there was no sin', and so undoes the world grievance by grievance.
figures.facet: Forgiveness inverted from transaction to demolition: it does not excuse a real offense but unmakes the offense's reality — mercy as metaphysical solvent rather than moral generosity.
figures.provenance: A Course in Miracles, Workbook lessons on forgiveness and Text chapter 26; Kenneth Wapnick, 'Forgiveness and Jesus' (1983).

figures.gloss: One lesson a day for 365 days — beginning at 'Nothing I see... means anything' — practiced alone and rehearsed in the living-room study groups that became the movement's whole congregational form; there is no ACIM church, clergy, or sacrament.
figures.facet: A religion with the institutional footprint of a book club: the curriculum itself is the institution, and the only ordination is having done the year.
figures.provenance: Workbook for Students (Foundation for Inner Peace, 1976); FIP and Miracle Distribution Center study-group networks; Marianne Williamson, 'A Return to Love' (1992).

figures.gloss: The Course's namesake unit of change: not a physical intervention but a shift in perception from fear to love, which the Text's opening fifty principles define as 'a correction' with 'no order of difficulty' between one miracle and another.
figures.facet: It relocates the miraculous entirely inside perception — nothing physical need move for a miracle to occur, and a physical event alone (a healing, a coincidence) is never sufficient to count as one.
figures.provenance: A Course in Miracles, Text, Chapter 1 ('Principles of Miracles') (Foundation for Inner Peace, 1976).

figures.gloss: The Columbia University medical psychologist (1923-1988) and Schucman's department head whose 1965 declaration 'there must be another way' is the attested trigger for the dictation; he typed her shorthand notes into the Text, Workbook, and Manual across the full seven years.
figures.facet: The co-originator written out of his own origin story: the scribing tradition remembers 'Schucman and the Voice,' but the Voice began with his plea and survived only because he sat at the typewriter for a decade.
figures.provenance: Kenneth Wapnick, 'Absence from Felicity' (1991); Foundation for Inner Peace scribal history; William Thetford's own accounts of the 1965 decision.

figures.gloss: The clinical psychologist (1942-2013) who worked with Schucman and Thetford on the final editing of the manuscript into publishable form, then co-founded the Foundation for A Course in Miracles and produced the movement's dominant body of exegesis, teaching tapes, and books.
figures.facet: He converts a private dictation into an institution twice over: first as its editor, fixing the text that exists, then as its chief interpreter, fixing the reading that most students inherit.
figures.provenance: Kenneth Wapnick, 'Absence from Felicity' (1991); Foundation for A Course in Miracles publication history; D. Patrick Miller's documentary histories of the Course.

figures.gloss: The author-lecturer whose 'A Return to Love' (1992) translated Course language ('our deepest fear is not that we are inadequate...') into a mass-market self-help idiom, and whose subsequent Oprah Winfrey Show appearances carried it into the American mainstream.
figures.facet: She is the tradition's popularizer rather than its interpreter: where Wapnick fixed a reading for the study-group world, Williamson supplied the sentence-length version the wider public actually knows.
figures.provenance: Marianne Williamson, 'A Return to Love' (HarperCollins, 1992); contemporary press coverage of the 1992 Oprah appearances.

figures.gloss: The present moment, chosen now, in which the mind sets aside the ego's narrative of time and accepts the Holy Spirit's judgment instead — time's purpose fulfilled in an instant rather than through a process.
figures.facet: The experiential hinge between the Text's theory and the Workbook's daily drill: it is where 'the Miracle' and 'Forgiveness' actually happen, the practice-unit in which the doctrine is cashed out moment to moment.
figures.provenance: A Course in Miracles, Text, Chapter 15 ('The Holy Instant') (Foundation for Inner Peace, 1976).

edges.note: The same event on both sides of the seam: the channeling shard holds the dictating Voice; this shard holds the scribe, the curriculum, and the community it produced.

Both dissolve evil into epistemology — a false belief with no ontological standing, cured by corrected mind rather than combat; the Course adds a psychoanalytic account of why the error defends itself.
Both universalize 'Christ' from a person into a state or function present in every mind, demoting the historical Jesus to its clearest demonstrator.
The text's own authorship claim: the dictating Voice is identified as Jesus and speaks in the first person about his crucifixion — attested as the tradition's self-understanding, not as history.
Co-originators of the scribing: his 1965 plea for 'another way' preceded the dictation and he typed her shorthand for the full seven years.
Edited the manuscript into publishable form with Schucman and then produced the exegesis that fixed how most study groups read it.
Her 1992 bestseller and Oprah appearances carried the study-group culture into the American mainstream, anchoring the tradition's own peak-period metadata.
The Miracle is forgiveness performed: both name the single shift from the ego's fear-based perception to the Holy Spirit's love-based one.
The Holy Instant is the moment in which the mind accepts the Holy Spirit's interpretation of events in place of the ego's.
The same scribed text; New Age culture at large excerpts it as free-floating spiritual psychology, apart from the dedicated Course-in-Miracles movement's disciplined daily-workbook practice.

ACIM keeps the Christian name and Trinity-adjacent function but re-metaphysicizes the Holy Spirit as the healed mind's inner Voice within an idealist cosmology where body and world are unreal — same name, sharply reworked ontology.

dossier.essence: A Course in Miracles is a modern channeled scripture set down between October 1965 and September 1972 by Helen Schucman, a research psychologist at Columbia University's College of Physicians and Surgeons, who took rapid inner dictation from a Voice the text identifies as Jesus while her colleague William Thetford typed her shorthand. Published in 1976 by the Foundation for Inner Peace, it has sold millions of copies and is studied worldwide in informal groups; its central claim is that the world of separation is an illusion made by the ego and undone through forgiveness.
dossier.cosmos: The metaphysics is strictly non-dual: only God and the creation God extends are real, while time, space, bodies, and death are a defensive dream manufactured by the ego — the belief in separation — to keep God seemingly absent. The Holy Spirit, God's Answer placed within the dreaming mind, reinterprets every event as a lesson leading home, and the Atonement is the undoing of the separation rather than payment for sin. Death belongs to the dream; the end is not travel to a heaven but awakening to a reality never actually left.
dossier.practice: There is no church, clergy, or sacrament: the curriculum is the institution. Practice is the self-study year — the Workbook's 365 daily lessons, one per day — alongside the Text and the Manual for Teachers, rehearsed socially in living-room study groups coordinated by bodies like the Foundation for Inner Peace and the Miracle Distribution Center. What it asks is the moment-by-moment application of forgiveness, understood not as pardoning a real offense but as recognizing that no offense ever occurred in reality.
dossier.canon: A Course in Miracles — Text, Workbook for Students, and Manual for Teachers (Foundation for Inner Peace, 1976), with two later scribed supplements, 'Psychotherapy: Purpose, Process and Practice' and 'The Song of Prayer'.
dossier.reflects: Revelation composed in the therapist's idiom — the impulse to have scripture and psychology certify each other.
sources: Foundation for Inner Peace, 'A Course in Miracles' (1976)
Kenneth Wapnick, 'Absence from Felicity: The Story of Helen Schucman and Her Scribing of A Course in Miracles' (1991)
Wouter J. Hanegraaff, 'New Age Religion and Western Culture: Esotericism in the Mirror of Secular Thought' (1996)
